questions.to_find: The return value of f(5) given the function with a static variable r.

understand.plain: The function f uses a static variable r that persists across all recursive calls (it is shared, not per-call). When n > 3, r is set to n and recursion continues with n-2. When n <= 3, the function adds r (the last set static value) to the recursive call. Tracing the call chain carefully with the shared r is the key.

keywords.explain: A local variable declared with 'static' retains its value between function calls; all invocations share the same storage.
keywords.example: static int r = 0; — r keeps its value even after the function returns.

keywords.explain: A function calling itself with a modified argument, building up a chain of deferred computations that unwind to produce the result.
keywords.example: f(5) calls f(3), which calls f(2), which calls f(1), which calls f(0).

understand.visual_alt: Call chain: f(5) sets r=5 and calls f(3); f(3) calls f(2) and adds r=5; f(2) calls f(1) and adds r=5; f(1) calls f(0) and adds r=5; f(0) returns 1. Unwinding: f(1)=6, f(2)=11, f(3)=16, f(5)=16+2=18.

given.aim: Find f(5) for the given recursive C function with a static variable.

terms.meaning: A single shared integer that persists and is visible to all recursive calls of f.
terms.example: Once set to 5 inside f(5), every subsequent call sees r=5.
terms.connects: When n<=3, each call adds r to the result, so its value directly scales the answer.

terms.meaning: Sets r = n, then recurses with n-2 and adds 2.
terms.connects: This is the only place r gets assigned, so r ends up as 5 for the entire chain.

terms.meaning: Returns f(n-1) + r, using the current static value of r.
terms.connects: Three additions of r=5 contribute 15 total; base f(0)=1 adds 1; the +2 from f(5) brings total to 18.

given.plan: Trace the call tree top-down, note that r is set to 5 the moment f(5) executes and never changes again. Then unwind from the base case f(0)=1 upward, adding r=5 at each level, and finally add 2 for the f(5) frame.

steps.title: Evaluate f(5) — set r and recurse
steps.apply: f(5) enters the 'n>3' branch: r is assigned 5. It then calls f(3) and will add 2 to whatever f(3) returns.
steps.note: r is now 5 for all subsequent calls — this is the crucial static side-effect.

steps.title: Evaluate f(3), f(2), f(1), f(0) — unwind with r=5
steps.note: All three levels add the same r=5 because r never changes during these calls.

steps.title: Return to f(5)
steps.note: The +2 is from the 'n>3' branch formula, not from r.

questions.formula_note: Static variable semantics in C recursion: a static local variable is allocated once and shared across all invocations, so assignment in one call is visible in all others.

questions.to_find: A value q (to 2 decimal places) such that f(q) returns approximately sqrt(3) ~ 1.73.

understand.plain: The function f uses Newton's method to find the square root of 3. It iterates the formula x_new = x/2 + 1.5/x = (x^2 + 3)/(2x), which converges to sqrt(3) when started from any positive number. The base condition checks if x is close enough to sqrt(3) (i.e. |x^2 - 3| < 0.01). We need to provide a starting value that is itself already close enough to sqrt(3) so that f returns immediately — OR any starting value that converges to sqrt(3).

keywords.explain: An iterative algorithm for finding square roots. For sqrt(3), the iteration is x_{n+1} = (x_n + 3/x_n) / 2, equivalent to x/2 + 1.5/x here.
keywords.example: Starting at x=2: x_1 = 1 + 0.75 = 1.75; x_2 ≈ 1.732 — very close to sqrt(3).

keywords.explain: The recursion stops when |x^2 - 3| < 0.01, meaning x is within about 0.005 of sqrt(3) ≈ 1.7320508.

understand.visual_alt: The iteration x_new = x/2 + 1.5/x is Heron's method for sqrt(3). It converges quadratically to 1.7320508... Any initial value q with |q^2 - 3| < 0.01 causes immediate return; otherwise the iteration converges to sqrt(3).

given.aim: Find a value q such that f(q) returns sqrt(3) (approximately 1.73).

terms.meaning: Newton-Raphson iteration for solving x^2 = 3, i.e., finding sqrt(3).
terms.connects: The fixed point of this iteration is x = sqrt(3), which is the value f ultimately returns.

terms.meaning: Termination test: stops recursion when x is close enough to sqrt(3).
terms.connects: Any q satisfying this condition causes f(q) to return q directly, which is approximately sqrt(3).

terms.meaning: The mathematical square root of 3, which is the value the recursion converges to.
terms.connects: The question asks for q such that f(q) returns this value; q = 1.73 satisfies |1.73^2-3|=0.0071<0.01.

given.plan: Identify the fixed point of the iteration: it is sqrt(3). Then find a starting value q where |q^2-3| < 0.01 so f returns immediately, or note that virtually any q converges to 1.73. The answer is 1.73.

steps.title: Identify what f computes
steps.apply: This is exactly Newton's method for sqrt(3): the fixed point satisfies x = x/2 + 3/(2x) => 2x = x + 3/x => x^2 = 3 => x = sqrt(3) ≈ 1.7320508.
steps.note: The function will always converge to and return a value near sqrt(3) regardless of the starting point (for positive x).

steps.title: Find q such that f(q) returns immediately (base case satisfied)
steps.apply: Try q = 1.73: 1.73^2 = 2.9929; |2.9929 - 3| = 0.0071 < 0.01. The condition is satisfied, so f(1.73) returns 1.73 directly.
steps.note: Any q in roughly [1.725, 1.739] satisfies the base condition. The standard answer given is 1.73.

solution.result: q = 1.73 (f(1.73) returns 1.73, which is approximately sqrt(3)). Answer: 1.73

questions.formula_note: Newton-Raphson / Heron's method for computing square roots: x_{n+1} = (x_n + S/x_n)/2 converges to sqrt(S). Here S=3, iteration x/2 + 1.5/x converges to sqrt(3) ≈ 1.73.

questions.to_find: The output printed by foo("ABCD EFGH").

understand.plain: The function foo recurses deeper into the string as long as the current character is not a null terminator and not a space. When it hits a space (or end of string), it simply returns without printing. On the way back out of the recursion it prints each character with putchar. Because recursion goes forward first and printing happens on the return, the non-space characters before the first space are printed in reverse order.

keywords.explain: Printing after the recursive call means the deepest character is printed first when unwinding — this reverses the order.
keywords.example: For 'AB': foo('A') calls foo('B'); foo('B') calls foo(' ') which returns immediately; then putchar('B') then putchar('A') — prints 'BA'.

keywords.explain: The condition *a != ' ' stops the recursion at the first space, so only the first word's characters are processed.
keywords.example: Input 'ABCD EFGH': recursion reaches 'A','B','C','D' then stops at ' ', never touching 'EFGH'.

understand.visual_alt: foo is called with 'A', 'B', 'C', 'D' in sequence (call phase). At ' ' it returns immediately. On the way back (return phase): putchar('D'), putchar('C'), putchar('B'), putchar('A'). Output: DCBA.

given.aim: Determine what foo prints when called with the string 'ABCD EFGH'.

terms.meaning: Recursion continues only if the current character is not null (end of string) and not a space.
terms.example: At 'A','B','C','D' the condition is true; at ' ' it is false — function returns without doing anything.
terms.connects: This means only 'ABCD' enters the recursive chain; 'EFGH' is never reached.

terms.meaning: The recursive call happens BEFORE printing, so printing occurs during the return (unwinding) phase.
terms.example: foo('A') first calls foo('B'), which calls foo('C')... all the way; only then prints starting from the deepest.
terms.connects: This reversal is why the output is 'DCBA' instead of 'ABCD'.

terms.meaning: A string with four characters before a space and four after.
terms.connects: The space stops recursion after D, so only ABCD is processed (in reverse).

given.plan: Trace the call stack: foo recurses forward through ABCD, halts at the space, then prints on the way back in reverse order: D, C, B, A.

steps.title: Trace the call phase (going in)
steps.apply:
foo('A'...): *a='A' — calls foo('B'...)
foo('B'...): *a='B' — calls foo('C'...)
foo('C'...): *a='C' — calls foo('D'...)
foo('D'...): *a='D' — calls foo(' '...)
foo(' '...): *a=' ' — condition false, returns immediately (no print).
steps.note: The recursion stops at the space and never touches 'EFGH'.

steps.title: Trace the return phase (unwinding) — printing occurs here
steps.apply:
foo('D'...) resumes: putchar('D') — prints D
foo('C'...) resumes: putchar('C') — prints C
foo('B'...) resumes: putchar('B') — prints B
foo('A'...) resumes: putchar('A') — prints A
Final output: DCBA
steps.note: Since printing is post-recursion, the last character reached is printed first — a classic string-reversal pattern.

solution.result: The output is DCBA. Answer: D. DCBA

questions.formula_note: Post-order recursive traversal pattern: calling recursively before performing the operation (putchar) reverses the processing order. Stopping at a delimiter (space) limits the portion of the string processed.
